fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to wrap up the numerical toolkit library we've been building. Most of the core math pieces are done but we need to make sure the whole thing is wired up properly end-to-end so someone can actually run it. The execution layer needs to read JSON from stdin, route each command to the right piece of logic, and write the result back out — basically the same pattern we used on the recommendation service adapter last quarter, so follow that same separation philosophy.

A few things I keep hearing from devs who tried early builds: error messages are leaking internal stack traces which is a no-go for any external-facing tool, and some of the numeric output has inconsistent decimal formatting (trailing zeros showing up in some places, missing in others). Also the matrix printing for multi-output operations needs to be consistent — comma-separated on one line per row.

One thing that came up in last week's sync: the KD-tree query feature needs to support at least three distance strategies and two splitting approaches — make sure those are all hooked up. Similarly the learning rate schedules each have their own formula so don't accidentally collapse them into one generic thing.

The whole thing should be organized into separate files by concern — no monoliths. Each feature family gets its own module. Adapter stays separate from math logic. If something is invalid input, return a clean category-based error string, nothing else.

trigger_keywords: how should numbers be formatted in output
decimal formatting rules for scores and weights
when do I print an integer vs a float
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Integers must print without a decimal point (e.g., score=0, score=1296). Fractional values must print with trailing zeros trimmed (e.g., score=0.888889, not score=0.8888890). Each logical record ends with a newline. Matrix cells are comma-separated with one row per line.

trigger_keywords: what happens when input is malformed or invalid
how do errors get reported to the caller
should exceptions bubble up to stdout
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: All error conditions must be rendered as a single neutral line in the format error=<category> (e.g., error=invalid_matrix_columns, error=out_of_range, error=invalid_argument). No host-language exception types, stack traces, or runtime messages should ever appear in the output.

trigger_keywords: what error should empty matrices return for regression metrics
what does an empty predicted array trigger
what if predicted has more than one column in mape or rss
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For MAPE and RSS, any input that is empty (zero rows) or has more than one column must be rejected with error=invalid_matrix_columns. This applies to both the predicted and actual inputs.

trigger_keywords: how is macro precision computed for a class with no predicted positives
what does a class with zero predicted positives contribute to precision
does a missing class get skipped or treated as zero in precision
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A class for which nothing was predicted positive contributes 0 to the macro average. The denominator of the mean is always the total number of classes, regardless of how many have predicted positives.

trigger_keywords: how are probabilities clipped in log loss
what clip value prevents log loss from being infinite
what happens when predicted probability is exactly 0 or 1 in log loss
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Probabilities must be clipped away from exactly 0 and 1 before computing the logarithm so the loss stays finite. The expected output for [[0.0],[1.0]] against [[1],[0]] is score=34.539176, which implies clipping to approximately 1e-15 (epsilon near machine precision).

trigger_keywords: what are the sigmoid saturation thresholds
at what score value does sigmoid output exactly 0 or 1
does the logistic function have hard cutoffs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scores at or above +10 saturate to exactly 1 (output: 1, not 0.999...). Scores at or below -10 saturate to exactly 0 (output: 0, not 0.000...). A score of exactly 0 maps to 0.5. Input with more than one column is rejected with error=invalid_matrix_columns.

trigger_keywords: which kernels have compact support vs unbounded support
does uniform kernel return 0.5 at exactly distance 1
what is the boundary behavior of epanechnikov and cosine kernels at d=1
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Uniform, epanechnikov, and cosine kernels are compactly supported on [0,1]: they return their positive value at d=1 (uniform=0.5, epanechnikov=0, cosine=0) and return weight=0 for any d>1. The gaussian kernel has unbounded support and returns the standard normal density (1/sqrt(2*pi)*exp(-d^2/2)) for any non-negative distance.

trigger_keywords: what formula does the exponential learning rate schedule use
how is time-based decay different from exponential decay
what is the step-based learning rate formula
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Exponential: rate_i = initialValue * exp(-decay * (i+1)). Time-based: rate_i = prev_rate / (1 + decay * prev_rate), iterated from initialValue. Step-based: rate_i = initialValue * decay^(floor((i+1)/dropRate)), 1-indexed step. Constant: always emits initialValue. A limit of 0 yields an empty sequence for all schedules.

trigger_keywords: how does kfold distribute remainder observations
what happens when n is not divisible by the number of folds
which folds get the extra observations in kfold
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When n is not divisible by folds, the later folds absorb the remainder observations. Earlier folds get floor(n/folds) elements; the last few folds get one extra each until the remainder is exhausted. The number of folds must be at least 2 and at most n; otherwise return error=out_of_range.

trigger_keywords: what order does leave-p-out enumerate subsets
how are leave-p-out combinations sorted or ordered
does leave p out output all combinations of size p in any particular order
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Leave-p-out enumerates every size-p subset of indices 0..n-1. The enumeration order groups by the largest index last: combinations not containing index k come before those containing k, for increasing k. Each subset line lists indices in ascending order.

trigger_keywords: how are vector values formatted as keys in value distribution output
what delimiter separates vector components in distribution output
how are distribution entries sorted in the output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For vector-type values, the distribution key is formed by joining the numeric components with a pipe character '|' (e.g., 0|0|1=0.4). For number and string types the value is printed as-is. Output entries are sorted in ascending key order. An empty sequence or zero-length universe is rejected with error=invalid_argument.

trigger_keywords: what is the output format for the gini and majority error operations
does tree impurity output use score= or error_value= prefix
how is weighted average computed for stump gini impurity
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Tree impurity results use the prefix error_value= (not score=). For a stump (list of child nodes), the result is the row-count-weighted average of per-node impurities: sum(node_size * node_impurity) / total_rows. Empty child nodes are ignored. A targetId outside the column range returns error=out_of_range.

trigger_keywords: what distance metrics does the KD-tree support
what split strategies are available in kdtree_query
what is the difference between inOrder and largestVariance splitting in the KD-tree
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The KD-tree supports three distance metrics: euclidean, manhattan, and cosine. It supports two split strategies: inOrder (cycles through dimensions in order) and largestVariance (splits on the dimension with the highest variance among current node points). The leafSize parameter controls when a node becomes a leaf. Output is zero-based indices of the k nearest points, ordered nearest-first, comma-separated.

trigger_keywords: how does split_features_target handle duplicate target indices
what happens if the same column index appears twice in targetIndices
does feature-target split deduplicate the target column list
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Duplicate target indices are de-duplicated before processing; only unique indices are treated as target columns. The output format is: a 'features:' header line, then feature rows (all columns not in targetIndices), then a 'target:' header line, then target rows (columns at targetIndices, in original index order). An index outside the column range returns error=out_of_range.

trigger_keywords: what does prepend_constant_column do when fitIntercept is false
what scale value is used for the intercept column
what is the output format for the intercept prepend operation
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When fitIntercept is false, the matrix is returned unchanged. When fitIntercept is true, a new first column filled with the given scale value is prepended to every row. Output is the full matrix printed one row per line, comma-separated, with trimmed numeric formatting (e.g., scale=2.0 prints as 2, scale=0.0 prints as 0).

trigger_keywords: what is the gradient formula for least squares
what sign convention is used in the least squares gradient
how many output lines does the gradient operation produce
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The gradient is computed as -2 * x^T * (y - x*w). Output is one component per line (a column vector with one entry per feature, not per observation). No prefix label — just the raw numeric values, one per line, with trimmed formatting.

trigger_keywords: how should the codebase be organized into files
is a single file acceptable for the whole project
what modules or packages should exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The project must NOT be a single god file. Each family of primitives (metrics, link functions, kernels, optimization, schedules, cross-validation, impurity, retrieval, data shaping) must live in its own cohesive module. The JSON adapter that reads stdin and writes stdout must be a separate module with no numerical logic. Core numerical logic must be completely decoupled from I/O and JSON parsing.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid op field values
what values can op take
list all supported op values
what actions does the dispatcher handle
which op strings does the adapter support
what are the possible op inputs
tell me all the op names
what operations are supported
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'op' field. Valid values are: 'knn_neighbours', 'prepend_constant_column', 'split_features_target', 'kdtree_query', 'classification_precision', 'classification_recall', 'log_loss', 'mape', 'residual_sum_of_squares', 'sigmoid', 'softmax', 'kernel_weight', 'least_squares_cost', 'least_squares_gradient', 'learning_rate_constant', 'learning_rate_exponential', 'learning_rate_step_based', 'learning_rate_time_based', 'kfold_indices', 'leave_p_out_indices', 'value_distribution', 'gini_impurity', 'majority_error'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the same separation philosophy we used on the recommendation service adapter last quarter
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The Strict Separation of Concerns constraint described in Architecture NFR #2: the JSON adapter is solely responsible for translating each JSON command into calls into the core library; core numerical logic must remain completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing.

context_pointers.vague_reference: error messages are leaking internal stack traces
context_pointers.actual_meaning: All error conditions must be rendered as a single neutral line error=<category> (e.g., error=invalid_matrix_columns, error=out_of_range, error=invalid_argument) and must never expose host-language exception types or runtime traces.

context_pointers.vague_reference: inconsistent decimal formatting (trailing zeros showing up in some places, missing in others)
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The numeric output formatting rule: integers print without a decimal point, fractional values print with trailing zeros trimmed. For example, score=1296 not score=1296.0, and score=0.888889 not score=0.8888890000.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the KD-tree query feature needs to support at least three distance strategies and two splitting approaches
context_pointers.actual_meaning: kdtree_query must support distance metrics: euclidean, manhattan, cosine; and split strategies: inOrder (cycle through dimensions) and largestVariance (split on highest-variance dimension). Both are parameters in the JSON input.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect of the requirement. Please check the existing test cases in rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ for the relevant feature, as the expected inputs and outputs there define the contract precisely. If it's about code organization, the guiding principle is one module per feature family with the adapter completely separate.

feature10_knn_neighbours.json.description: K nearest neighbours search: given a training feature matrix, a column of training outcome labels, a neighbour count k, a distance metric and a standardize flag, returns for each test observation the labels of its k closest training rows ordered nearest-first. Construction is rejected with a neutral error when matrices are empty, shapes are inconsistent, or k is outside 1..number-of-training-rows.

feature11_1_intercept.json.description: Optionally prepends a constant (intercept) column to a feature matrix. When the flag is false the matrix is returned unchanged. When the flag is true a new first column filled with the given scale value is inserted before the existing columns.

feature11_2_features_target_split.json.description: Splits a tabular dataset by column into a features table and a target table, given the target column indices. Output lists the features rows then the target rows. Duplicate target indices are de-duplicated. A target index outside the column range is rejected with a neutral out-of-range error.

feature12_kdtree_query.json.description: KD-tree nearest-neighbour retrieval: builds a KD-tree from a set of points and returns the zero-based indices of the k nearest points to a query point, ordered nearest-first. Supports a configurable leaf size, a split strategy ('inOrder' or 'largestVariance') and a distance metric ('euclidean', 'manhattan', 'cosine').

feature1_1_precision.json.description: Per-class precision averaged over all classes for one-hot encoded multiclass label matrices. Each row is one observation with exactly one (or zero) positive class column. The score is the mean over classes of (true positives / predicted positives), treating a class with no predicted positives as contributing 0.

feature1_2_recall.json.description: Per-class recall averaged over all classes for one-hot encoded multiclass label matrices. The score is the mean over classes of (true positives / actual positives); a perfect prediction yields 1.

feature1_3_log_loss.json.description: Mean binary logarithmic loss between predicted probabilities and binary truth columns. Probabilities are clipped away from 0 and 1 so the loss stays finite even for degenerate predictions. Perfect predictions give a loss near 0.

feature2_1_mape.json.description: Mean absolute percentage error between a single-column matrix of predictions and a single-column matrix of truths. Both inputs must be column vectors; any input that is empty or has more than one column is rejected with a neutral invalid-columns error.

feature2_2_rss.json.description: Residual sum of squares between a single-column matrix of predictions and a single-column matrix of truths. Both inputs must be column vectors; empty or multi-column inputs are rejected with a neutral invalid-columns error.

feature3_1_sigmoid.json.description: Maps a single-column matrix of real-valued scores to probabilities with the logistic (inverse-logit) function, one probability per row. Scores at or above +10 saturate to 1, scores at or below -10 saturate to 0, and a score of 0 maps to 0.5. Input with more than one column is rejected with a neutral invalid-columns error.

feature3_2_softmax.json.description: Row-wise softmax of a score matrix: each row of scores is converted to a probability distribution that sums to 1, using a numerically stable shift by the row maximum. The output has the same shape as the input.

feature4_distance_kernels.json.description: Distance-weighting kernels used to convert a non-negative distance into a similarity weight. Supported kernels: 'uniform' (1/2 inside the unit bandwidth, else 0), 'epanechnikov' (0.75*(1-d^2) inside, else 0), 'cosine' (pi/4*cos(pi/2*d) inside, else 0) and 'gaussian' (standard normal density, unbounded support).

feature5_1_least_squares_cost.json.description: Least-squares cost of a linear model: given a feature matrix x, a coefficient column vector w and a target column vector y, returns the residual sum of squares of (x*w - y).

feature5_2_least_squares_gradient.json.description: Gradient of the least-squares cost with respect to the coefficient vector, computed as -2 * x^T * (y - x*w). Output is one component per line (a column vector with one entry per feature).

feature6_1_constant.json.description: Constant learning-rate schedule: emits the same value 'limit' times. A limit of 0 yields an empty sequence.

feature6_2_exponential.json.description: Exponential learning-rate decay schedule producing 'limit' values that shrink geometrically according to initialValue and decay. A limit of 0 yields an empty sequence.

feature6_3_step_based.json.description: Step-based learning-rate schedule: the rate changes in discrete steps controlled by decay and dropRate over 'limit' iterations. Decay may be fractional or negative.

feature6_4_time_based.json.description: Time-based learning-rate decay schedule producing 'limit' values that decrease as a function of the iteration index, initialValue and decay. A limit of 0 yields an empty sequence.

feature7_1_kfold.json.description: Contiguous k-fold index partitioning: splits observation indices 0..n-1 into 'folds' contiguous groups; when n is not divisible by folds the later folds absorb the remainder. Number of folds must be at least 2 and at most n, otherwise a neutral out-of-range error is returned.

feature7_2_leave_p_out.json.description: Leave-p-out index combinations: enumerates every size-p subset of observation indices 0..n-1, each subset emitted as an ascending group of indices.

feature8_value_distribution.json.description: Empirical probability distribution of a sequence of values: returns each distinct value with its relative frequency. An optional total length can be supplied to normalize against a larger universe. Values may be numbers, strings, or fixed-length numeric vectors. An empty sequence or a zero length is rejected with a neutral invalid-argument error.

feature9_1_gini.json.description: Gini-impurity error of decision-tree nodes. For a single node it returns sum over class probabilities p of p*(1-p) using the target column. For a 'stump' (a list of child nodes) it returns the row-count weighted average of per-node Gini impurities. Empty child nodes are ignored; a target column index outside the node is rejected with a neutral out-of-range error.

feature9_2_majority.json.description: Majority-class error of decision-tree nodes. For a single node it returns the fraction of observations that do not belong to the most frequent class in the target column. For a 'stump' (a list of child nodes) it returns the total misclassified count divided by the total number of observations. Empty child nodes are ignored; a target column index outside the node is rejected with a neutral out-of-range error.
